segments.s069-g1: Literal (poetic) word order: the preposition 'به' (to/for) marks the recipients of what 'reached.' 'مهراب و دستان' are the two proper names in the dative role; the verb 'رسید' (reached/arrived) comes after them, and the demonstrative 'این' + noun 'سخن' (this speech/news) is the thing that arrived. In older/poetic Persian the thing that arrives often follows the indirect object as here.
segments.s069-g2: The subordinating particle 'که' introduces reported content ('that ...'). 'شاه و سپهبد' are the compound subject, joined by 'و'. 'فگندند' is an archaic/poetic past plural verb (equivalent to classical 'فکندند' / 'فراکندند') meaning 'they cast/threw' and takes the following noun 'بن' as its object. Word order is SOV in the subordinate clause (subject — object — verb).
segments.s069-g3: 'خروشان' is a present participial/adverbial adjective meaning 'roaring/with a roar' and precedes the rest of the clause as a manner adjunct. 'ز کابل' is the source ('from Kabul') using the poetic preposition 'ز'. 'همی رفت' is an archaic continuous past construction (همی + past stem) meaning 'was going' or here more idiomatically 'set off / went', and 'زال' is the subject at the end (typical of older Persian: manner/place/verb precede the subject).
segments.s069-g4: Two adjectival phrases describe Zal's appearance: 'فروهشته لفج' — 'فروهشته' is a past‑participle/adjective meaning 'scattered' or 'tousled' modifying the following noun 'لفج' (an obscure/poetic term here left untranslated except as a descriptive element). The conjunction 'و' links that phrase to 'برآورده یال', where 'برآورده' is a past participle used adjectivally meaning 'raised/uplifted' and 'یال' (mane) is its head noun. Adjectives/past‑participles commonly precede the noun they modify in this diction.
segments.s069-g5: 'همی گفت' is a frequentative/continuous past expression meaning 'he was saying' or 'he kept saying.' 'اگر' introduces a conditional clause ('if'). 'اژدهای دژم' is the noun phrase 'the grim dragon' where 'دژم' is an attributive adjective 'grim/sullen' placed after the noun (a typical poetic ordering).
segments.s069-g6: 'بیاید' is the present/subjunctive form of 'آمدن' used in a protasis ('should come' / 'comes') following 'اگر.' The particle 'که' introduces the apodosis (result clause). 'گیتی' is the patient ('the world'), and 'بسوزد' is a subjunctive/optative verbal form meaning 'would burn' / 'be burned.' 'به دم' is an instrumental phrase 'by/with the breath' (here 'به' has the sense 'by/‑ly' + 'دم' = 'breath'). Word order: conditional verb — result particle — object — verb — instrument.
segments.s069-g7: 'چو' here functions as a conditional 'if' (classical/poetic usage). 'کابلستان را' is the direct object marked by 'را' (definite/accusative). The verb 'بخواهد' (from خواستن) is in the present/future sense 'wants/desires,' and 'بسود' is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'to the advantage' (i.e. 'for the profit/benefit of'). Together they form the protasis: 'If he wants Kabulistan to his advantage...'.
segments.s069-g8: This clause states the required consequence. 'نخستین' ('first') modifies 'سر من' ('my head'). 'بباید' is an impersonal/modal auxiliary meaning 'must / ought to be' (here giving necessity). 'درود' in this context is used as 'offering/greeting' or 'being presented' — idiomatically 'to be offered/put forward' — so the phrase literally says 'my head must be the first to be offered.' Note the older Persian order: modifier + noun ('نخستین سر من') before the modal verb.
segments.s069-g9: به پیش + noun + شد is a classical sequence meaning “(he) went/stood before (someone).” Here به پیش پدر شد = “he came/stood before his father.” The following phrase پر از خون جگر attaches as a predicate: شد پر از خون جگر = “became/was full of the blood of jigar (i.e. his heart/liver).” Note the poetic noun-order and the use of شد as simple past linking verb.
segments.s069-g10: پر placed before a noun phrase means “full (of)”; پر اندیشه دل = “the heart full of thought.” The second half, پر ز گفتار سر, uses poetic ز (= از) + noun to indicate content: “the head full of speech.” The two verbless adjectival phrases describe his inner state and link back to the subject introduced earlier.
segments.s069-g11: چو here = “when.” آگاهی آمد is a verbal clause “news/information came.” به سام دلیر places the recipient after آمد with the preposition به: “the news came to Sam, the brave.” Delir (دلیر) is an epithet following the proper name.
segments.s069-g12: که introduces a subordinate/relative clause “that/which.” آمد is the past verb “came.” ز ره is the poetic prepositional phrase “from/along the road.” بچه نره شیر is a genitive noun phrase where بچه (offspring/cub) is qualified by نره شیر (a male lion); classical Persian often omits the ezāfe, so the phrase reads as “the cub of the male lion.”
segments.s069-g13: همه لشکر is the collective subject “the whole army/everyone of the host.” از جای = “from their places/positions.” برخاستند is the plural past verb “they rose/rose up,” agreeing with the plural sense of the subject.
segments.s069-g14: The possessed-before-possessor order (درفش فریدون) is classical: “Fereydun’s banner.” بیاراستند is the past plural verb “they adorned/arrayed,” so the clause means “they adorned Fereydun’s banner.”
segments.s069-g15: پذیره شدن is a deverbal noun phrase ‘the becoming/being received’ and را marks it as the definite object. تبیره زدند uses زدند (they struck/sounded) with تبیره as the thing struck/sounded; in context this means they made an announcement/proclamation for the reception. Word order is object را + verb, a common classical arrangement.
segments.s069-g16: سپاه و سپهبد is a coordinated compound subject “the army and the spahbed (general).” پذیره شدند is a passive or middle construction in the past plural: “were received” or “were accepted.” The clause states that both the army and the spahbed were received.
segments.s069-g89: بدو گفت is the common inverted word order 'to him he said' (بدو = 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb گفت). آری is an interjection ('yes/indeed'). همینست is the fused form of همین + است ('this is exactly so'); راست is a predicative adjective meaning 'true' placed after the copula phrase.
segments.s069-g90: زبان تو is the noun (زبان = 'speech/utterance') with the second-person possessor تو. بر راستی is a prepositional phrase 'with/regarding truth' (بر = 'on/with', راستی = 'truth'); گواست is an archaic/predicative form meaning 'is witness/testifies' (contracted from گواه است) and serves as the main verb of the clause.
segments.s069-g91: همه کار من is a nominal subject 'all my affairs' (همه modifies کار; من is the possessor). با تو is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with you'. بیداد بود is the predicate: بیداد (an abstract noun 'injustice/oppression') + بود 'was' — i.e. ‘amounted to injustice/was unjust.’
segments.s069-g92: دل دشمنان Literally 'the heart(s) of the enemies' (possessive noun phrase). بر تو marks the target/cause 'concerning/at you'. بر شاد بود is the predicate 'were glad' (شاد 'happy', بود 'was/were'); Persian often uses this singular construction with plural possessors in poetry.
segments.s069-g93: ز من is the source ('from me'). آرزو خود is the object with خود as a reflexive/emphatic 'your own/one's own desire'. همین qualifies it ('this very'). خواستی is past second-person 'you wanted/asked' and is the finite verb governing the clause.
segments.s069-g94: به تنگی دل is an ablative/causal phrase 'from/with the tightness/oppression of the heart' (بیانی of distress). از جای برخاستی is the action 'you rose from your place' (از = 'from', جای = 'place/seat', برخاستی past 'you rose'). The two phrases give cause and action.
segments.s069-g95: مشو تیز is a negative imperative 'do not become sharp/hasty' (مشو = 'do not become', تیز = 'sharp/quick/hasty'). تا introduces purpose/result 'so that/until', and چاره کار تو is the object phrase 'the remedy for your affair' (چاره 'remedy', کار تو 'your affair').
segments.s069-g96: بسازم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive used purposively here 'that I may make/arrange' (in sense of future action). کنون 'now' and نیز 'also' modify the verb; بازار تو ('your market') functions as the object idiomatically meaning your affairs/prospects that he will set right.
segments.s069-g137: The phrase puts a topical noun before the predicate: 'زمین' (the land/earth) is presented, then the predicate 'پهنای او' (its breadth). 'شهر تا شهر' uses 'تا' in the sense 'from ... to / as far as' to mark the horizontal extent (city to city). Persian omits the copula here, so the whole reads 'the land, from city to city, was its breadth.'
segments.s069-g138: 'همان' (= 'the same') links this clause to the previous; 'کوه تا کوه' repeats the same 'from ... to ...' extent pattern but vertically. 'بالای او' literally 'its top/above' functions here as 'its height.' Word order again places the extent before the predicate; the verb/copy is omitted.
segments.s069-g139: 'جهان را' uses the object/definite marker 'را' to topicalize 'the world.' 'ازو' (from/of him) gives the cause or source of the feeling. 'بود' is the past copula ('was'), and 'دل پر هراس' is the predicate 'a heart full of fear' — together: 'the world was, on account of him, a heart full of dread.'
segments.s069-g140: 'همی' is the habitual/continuous particle (poetic), and 'داشتندی' is an archaic/past plural form meaning 'they kept' (here 'kept/watch/held'). 'شب و روز' ('night and day') gives the temporal frame, and 'پاس' is a noun 'guard/watch' serving as the object: 'they kept watch night and day.'
segments.s069-g141: 'هوا پاک' ('the air/sky clean') is the descriptive phrase; 'دیدم' is first-person past 'I saw.' The preposition 'ز' (= 'from/of' in poetic usage) with 'پرندگان' indicates absence or removal ('free/cleared of birds'): 'I saw the air clear of birds.'
segments.s069-g142: 'همان' ('that same') ties back to the previous clause; 'روی گیتی' is a compound 'the face/aspect of the world.' 'ز درندگان' again uses 'ز' to indicate agency/source ('by/from the predators') — i.e. that aspect of the world belonged to or was characterized by predators.
segments.s069-g143: The preposition 'ز' ('from/because of') introduces cause: 'from his spit/breath (تفش)'. 'همی' marks the continuous/poetic aspect. 'پر کرگس' is a genitive phrase 'the vulture's feathers' (feathers of the vulture). 'بسوخت' is past passive/ intransitive 'were burned/ burned up' or 'were scorched.'
segments.s069-g144: 'زمین' (the earth/ground) is the affected locus; 'زیر زهرش' ('under his poison') places the earth beneath the effect of his venom. 'همی' again gives the habitual/continuous coloring, and 'برفروخت' here uses the poetic/older sense 'was kindled/inflamed' (not the commercial 'sold'): 'the earth was set aflame by his poison.'
segments.s069-g33: This is a compact conditional clause. پدر‌گر is best read as پدر + گر (گر = 'if'), an archaic conditional clitic attached to 'father' meaning 'if the father...'. به مغز اندر آرد خرد is word‑order typical of older Persian: به مغز اندر (into the mind/brain) + آرد (3rd‑person present/optative of آوردن, 'brings/puts') + خرد (object 'wisdom'). The verb refers back to the conditional subject ('the father').
segments.s069-g34: همانا is an emphatic adverb 'verily'. سخن بر سخن sets up a repetition/stacking image ('speech upon speech' / 'one utterance upon another'), where بر is the preposition 'on/upon'. نگذرد is negative present 3rd person (ن‑ + گذرد) meaning 'does not/will not pass' (here: 'one saying will not pass another').
segments.s069-g35: وگر is the conditional 'and if'. برگشاید is a present/optative 3rd‑person form of برگشودن 'to open/be loosed' ('should open/looses'). زبان را uses را to mark the definite object ('the tongue' / 'speech'); به خشم is a manner phrase 'in anger' (به + noun giving the sense 'with/in'). The clause means 'if the tongue is loosed in anger...'.
segments.s069-g36: پس introduces the result ('then' / 'thereupon'). از شرمش is a causal/ablative expression 'from/out of his (or my) shame'. آب اندر آرم به چشم is literal 'I put water into (my) eye(s)'—an idiom for shedding tears; اندر before the verb marks direction 'into'. آرم here is a first‑person verb form (archaic) meaning 'I put/bring' rather than the modern senses listed elsewhere.
segments.s069-g37: چنین 'thus/so' sets the scene. تا here functions like 'up to' / 'as far as' introducing the destination: تا به درگاه سام آمدند = 'they came up to / arrived at Sam's court'. آمدند is the past/pluperfective perfective 'they came/arrived'.
segments.s069-g38: Two adjectival predicates precede the verb: گشاده‌دل 'open‑hearted' and شادکام 'fortunate/joyful'; و simply coordinates them. آمدند repeats the verbal predicate 'they came' — a common poetic pattern where qualities are stated then the action 'came' closes the clause.
segments.s069-g39: فرود آمد is a compound verb meaning 'alighted / came down / dismounted'. از باره سام here means 'from Sam's retinue/charge/occasion' (بارة in classical usage can mean 'on the occasion or account of'); سوار is the noun 'rider/horseman' and functions as the subject that dismounts. Word order is poetic: verb phrase then source phrase then subject.
segments.s069-g40: هم 'also/and' links the action; اندر زمان 'at that time / in the moment' (اندر = in/within, زمان = moment here). زال را داد بار is a straightforward ditransitive sequence: زال را marks the indirect object 'to Zāl', داد is the past tense 'gave', and بار is the direct object 'the load/burden'.
segments.s069-g25: نشست is a past‑tense, third‑person singular verb ‘he sat/rode’ (not a noun). The prepositional sequence از بر is a classical phrasing meaning ‘onto/on the back of’ (از retains its basic ‘from/out of’ sense but here combines with بر to indicate mounting). تازی اسپ سمند is a string of epithets: تازی (Arabian), اسپ (steed/horse) and سمند (war‑horse/steed) piled after the preposition as the object of نشست.
segments.s069-g26: چو is the simile conjunction ‘like/as’. The following adjectives (زرین درخشنده) modify کوهی بلند: a noun (کوهی) with the adjective بلند, literally ‘a mountainy/towering one’ — here used metaphorically to describe the horse’s appearance. Word order is typical Persian: simile marker + modifiers + head noun + adjective.
segments.s069-g27: بزرگان همه is the noun phrase ‘all the great ones/elders’. پیش او آمدند uses پیش in the sense ‘in the presence of / before’ and آمدند is the past plural ‘they came’. The construction is straightforward S + adverbial + verb (the verb agrees plural).
segments.s069-g28: به تیمار و با گفت و گو آمدند is a coordinated adverbial phrase describing manner: به تیمار ‘with solicitude/care’ and با گفت‌وگو ‘with talk/conversation’; آمدند repeats the verb to emphasize their approaching attitude. Note گفت و گو is a fixed collocation ‘conversation’ formed by two parts (گفت and گو).
segments.s069-g29: که introduces an indirect statement ‘that …’. آزرده گشتست is a perfect/resultative phrase: آزرده (distressed/afflicted) + گشتست (has become) = ‘has become distressed’. بر تو indicates the target ‘toward/against you’, and پدر is the affected party ‘father’ (subject of the state).
segments.s069-g30: این یک جمله امری است: یکی (an indefinite ‘someone’) + پوزش آور (آور as a verb form ‘bring!’) is a call to bring an apology. مکش is a negative imperative (‘do not pull/do not do’), followed by هیچ سر literally ‘any head’ — idiomatically a prohibitive (here ‘do not let any head …’ or ‘do not bow/lower any head’); the terse classical imperative order omits explicit subjects.
segments.s069-g31: چنین داد پاسخ is an inverted verb phrase: چنین ‘thus’ + داد (past ‘gave’) + پاسخ ‘an answer’ (= ‘he answered thus’). کزین is a fused classical prepositional form ‘from this’ and باک نیست is an existential negation ‘there is no fear/concern’. The clause reports the content of his reply.
segments.s069-g32: سرانجام آخر is emphatic repetition meaning ‘finally/at last’. به جز + noun means ‘except’ (به = part of the idiom), so به جز خاک نیست literally ‘there is nothing except dust’. نیست is the copular/existential negation ‘is not/there is not’. Word order places adverbial emphasis before the predicate.
segments.s069-g65: ز (ablative) + مادر sets up 'from my mother'. بزادم is an archaic/periphrastic past-like form meaning 'I was born' (ب + زادم), placed before the verb as a temporal/grounding phrase. بینداختی is past, 2nd-person singular ('you threw' or 'you cast'); the prefixed بــ/بی‑ is a classical/poetic variant of the verb انداختی. Together: 'You threw me from my mother when I was born.'
segments.s069-g66: به کوه gives the target location ('into/to the mountain'); اندرم is an older locative/locution meaning 'inside/within (for me)'—here marking 'in the mountain' as the site for the speaker; جایگه 'place/position' is the noun being created and ساختی is past 2nd-person singular 'you made/constructed'. Word order is poetic (location phrase then verb).
segments.s069-g67: فگندی is an obscure/archaic past-form (cf. classical فکندی) meaning roughly 'you cast/sent/entrusted' and appears here as past 2nd-person. به تیمار 'to the care' marks the recipient/agency of that action; زاینده را is the definite object ('the lifegiver' or 'the begetter') with the object marker را. The whole clause states that you placed that person/thing into care.
segments.s069-g68: به آتش 'into/unto the fire' (به = toward/into) marks destination; سپردی is past 2nd-person of سپردن 'you entrusted/consigned'; فزاینده را is the object with را. The clause means 'you consigned the growing/increasing one to the fire.'
segments.s069-g69: ترا is the topical/accusative form 'you' (ت + را) placed up front for emphasis. با + جهان‌آفرین means 'with the world‑creator' (i.e. 'between you and the Creator'). نیست is the negative copula 'is not/there is not' and جنگ 'war' is the predicate. The clause literally reads 'There is no war between you and the world‑creator.'
segments.s069-g70: که introduces the following explanatory/relative clause; از چه here functions as 'because of what / why' (از = 'because of' + چه = 'what/why'). سیاه و سپیدست رنگ places the noun رنگ 'colour' at the end, with the adjectives before it: 'سیاه و سپید' (black and white) and the archaic -ست in سپیدست contains the copulative sense ('is/are'), so the phrase means 'that the colour is black and white.'
segments.s069-g71: کنون 'now' is an adverbial introducer. کم here modifies the idea of quantity/frequency ('few' / 'scarcely'), and جهان‌آفرین is the subject 'the world‑creator'; پرورید is a classical verb form meaning 'nurtured/has brought up' (here treated as past/perfective). The clause says, in effect, 'Now the Creator of the world has scarcely nurtured (any).' The compact word order (adverb + qualifier + subject + verb) is typical of classical verse.
segments.s069-g72: به چشم خدایی is an instrumental/attitudinal phrase 'with/by a godlike/divine eye' (به = by/with; خدایی = godlike/divine used adjectivally). به من 'at me' uses به as the preposition marking the target ('look at me'), and بنگرید is the polite/archaic 2nd‑person plural imperative/subjunctive of نگر (to look): 'look' or 'behold.' Together: 'Look upon me with a divine eye.'
segments.s069-g73: ابا is an archaic verb meaning “to be reluctant / to refuse” used here as a declarative (first-person) verb — “I did not spare / I did not refuse.” It takes a string of direct objects joined by و and prefaced by با before تخت: گنج، تخت، گرز گران are the coordinated objects (تخت and گرز modified by گران). The particle با here functions as a coordinating ‘with / and’ marking the next item in the series.
segments.s069-g74: This clause repeats the same archaic verb ابا (‘I refused / I would not withhold’) followed by another coordinated list. رای here means “counsel/plan”; the second با introduces the next group (تاج و تخت و سران) — ‘crown, throne, and chiefs’ — all objects coordinated to the verb. The clause therefore parallels the previous one: ‘I did not refuse counsel, nor crown, throne, and chiefs.’
segments.s069-g75: نشستم is the simple past (first-person) of نشستن — “I sat / I took my seat / I settled.” به کابل is a locative prepositional phrase ‘in / at Kabul’ (به here marking location/toward). به فرمان تو uses به with an instrumental/causal sense ‘by / at the command of you’ — ‘at your command.’ Word order is verb — locative — instrument/agent phrase.
segments.s069-g76: نگه داشتم is the compound verb نگه داشتن in the past tense (I kept/maintained/observed) — نگه is the noun/root ‘keeping; watch’ and داشتم is the past auxiliary ‘I had / I kept.’ رای و پیمان تو are the direct objects: ‘your counsel and your covenant/pledge.’ The phrase means ‘I upheld/kept your counsel and covenant.’
segments.s069-g77: که‌گر is a conditional conjunction meaning ‘if’ (that/if). کینه‌جویی is a deverbal noun ‘vengefulness / seeking revenge’ and is the subject/object of the condition. نیازارمت is an archaic negative verbal form meaning ‘I will not harm / I shall not afflict you’ (negative prefix نـ plus آزار with a 1st‑person ending/optative nuance). The clause reads as a conditional promise: ‘If you seek vengeance, I will not harm you.’
segments.s069-g78: درختی is ‘a tree’ used here metaphorically; که is the relative pronoun ‘that/which.’ کشتی is ‘ship’ and به بار is a prepositional phrase ‘for / to (be) laden with cargo’ (به marks purpose/recipient). آرمت is an archaic/poetic 1st‑person verb form (uncertain derivation in classical Persian) — the phrase as a whole is a compressed poetic image: ‘a tree which I would make/that would make a ship bear/load (cargo).’ The final verb is archaic/poetic and not a standard modern form.
segments.s069-g79: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from.’ مازندران is the place name ‘Mazandaran.’ هدیه این is ‘this gift’ (هدیه = gift, این = this). ساختی is the simple past, second-person singular of ساختن — ‘you made/you fashioned.’ Word order is poetic: ‘From Mazandaran you made this a gift.’
segments.s069-g80: هم is an adverb ‘also / likewise.’ از marks origin ‘from’; گرگساران is a plural/place‑name meaning either ‘wolf‑lands’ or ‘wolfish people / brigands’ — here a hostile region/people. بدین is the prepositional combination (ب + دین) meaning ‘to/against these’ (the demonstrative), and تاختی is the past second‑person singular of تاختن — ‘you charged / you rushed.’ The clause thus says: ‘and you likewise rushed from the wolf‑lands (upon them / upon these).’
segments.s069-g41: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning “when.” زال is the subject (the hero Zal). اندر آمد is a classical/perfective verb phrase “came in” (اندر + آمد), with اندر marking motion/entry. به پیش پدر is a prepositional phrase “into the presence of his father” (به + پیش + پدر = ‘to before father’), where پیش means “in the presence of.”
segments.s069-g42: زمین را is the definite direct object (زمین + object marker را) meaning “the earth.” ببوسید is the past third‑person singular “he kissed.” و coordinates two past actions. گسترد is an archaic past of گستردن “(he) spread / stretched (himself or something)”; بر is the preposition “upon/on” whose object is omitted in this poetic line (understood as the ground or himself).
segments.s069-g43: یکی here is indefinite “someone / a certain one.” آفرین کرد is the idiom “cried ‘afarin!’ / praised,” where آفرین is the exclamation and کردن (کرد) supplies the past tense. بر سام is the prepositional phrase “upon/for Sam.” گرد after Sam is an epithet: in this context it functions as an old poetic epithet attached to Sam (an archaic/epic adjective), not a literal preposition.
segments.s069-g44: وزاب is an obscure/name‑like item (likely a proper name) and begins the next clause. دو نرگس is “two narcissus (flowers)”—a common poetic image. همی is the old habitual/continuous marker here (“was / did repeatedly” or simply an emphatic/continuous particle). گل is ‘flower’ (object) and سترد is the past verb meaning here roughly “took/removed/plucked” in an archaic sense; that exact nuance is not covered by the listed senses, so the lexical choice is noted below.
segments.s069-g45: که introduces a clause expressing a wish/benediction (“that / may”). بیدار دل is a compound adjectival phrase “wakeful‑hearted” (بیدار modifying دل), followed by پهلوان “hero,” so بیدار دل پهلوان = “the hero with an awake heart.” شاد باد is an optative formula: شاد = “happy,” باد = optative particle “may (he be).”
segments.s069-g46: روانش is روان + enclitic ش “his soul/spirit.” گراینده is an agentive/participial noun “one who directs / the director.” داد is the noun “justice” (here the thing that should direct his soul). باد at the end is again the optative “may,” so the clause reads “May justice be the director/guide of his soul.”
segments.s069-g47: ز is the poetic preposition “from / by” indicating the agent/means. تیغ تو is a possessed noun phrase “your sword” (تیغ = sword, تو = you). الماس is the theme/subject “diamond(s).” بریان is a predicative adjective/participle “seared / roasted.” شود is the classical auxiliary/verb “become / shall be,” giving a passive/result meaning: “diamonds shall be seared by your sword.”
segments.s069-g48: زمین روز جنگ is a compressed, poetic locative phrase equivalent to “the earth on the day of battle” (روز جنگ modifies زمین; the clearer prose order would be در روز جنگ زمین). از تو gives the cause/agent “because of you / on your account.” گریان is a participial/adjectival form “weeping / causing to weep” and شود again is the auxiliary “shall become,” so the clause means “the earth shall be made to weep because of you on the day of battle.”
segments.s069-g17: همه modifies پشت پیلان and here means 'the whole/all of' — i.e. 'the entire backs of the elephants'. به introduces the following instrument/ornament ('with' a banner) and precedes the noun phrase رنگین درفش. Word order is the expected Persian sequence: noun phrase (subject) + prepositional/qualifying phrase.
segments.s069-g18: بیاراسته functions as a predicative participle/adjective meaning 'adorned/arrayed' (an archaic/classical usage here), linking back to the prepositional phrase 'به رنگین درفش' — i.e. 'were adorned with a colorful banner'. The sequence of color words (سرخ و زرد و بنفش) lists the hues of that ornament.
segments.s069-g19: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. روی پدر is the object phrase 'the father's face'. دید is the past-tense verb 'saw'. دستان سام here is a nominal phrase functioning as the subject performing the following actions; in this context دستان is best read as a name/epithet attached to سام (classical name usage), so the whole phrase names the actor 'Sam'.
segments.s069-g20: پیاده شد is a light-verb construction literally 'became on‑foot' = 'dismounted' (past). از اسپ gives the source 'from the steed/horse'. و joins the next action; بگذارد گام is an older/poetic phrasing meaning 'placed a step' — i.e. 'took a step' (بگذارد here corresponds to placing/taking a step).
segments.s069-g21: بزرگان is the plural subject 'the great ones/elders'. پیاده شدند is the past plural 'they dismounted' (literally 'became on foot'). از دو روی ('from both sides') specifies the direction/flanks — note in the word list دو was not repeated but the phrase means 'from both sides'.
segments.s069-g22: The correlative چه ... و چه ... here means 'whether ... or ...' or 'both ... and ...' linking two kinds of men; سالارخواه and سالارجوی are descriptive compounds meaning 'those who desire to be lord (power-seekers)' and 'those who seek the lordship/are aspirants', respectively. The coordinating و joins them.
segments.s069-g23: زمین را is the definite direct object 'the ground' marked by را. ببوسید is the past tense 'kissed'. زال دلیر is the subject phrase 'Zal the brave' placed after the verb (permissible word order in classical Persian poetry) and names the agent of the action.
segments.s069-g24: سخن گفت is a common compound meaning 'spoke' (literally 'said speech'); با او 'with him' marks the interlocutor. پدر نیز is 'the father also' (subject appearing after the verb, a frequent poetic order). دیر here is an adverb meaning 'for a long (time)' — so the clause says the father also spoke with him for a long while. (Note: some listed single‑word sense options for گفت did not include the verb sense, so the verb is treated as the past 'spoke' in context.)
segments.s069-g97: سخن order: 'یکی نامه فرمایم اکنون به شاه' places the thing being made ('یکی نامه' = 'a certain letter') before the verb. فرمایم is first-person present/subjunctive (classical/poetic) of فرمودن and here functions as 'I will compose / I shall say/compose' (a polite/poetic way to express the speaker's action). اکنون is a time adverb 'now' and به شاه is the prepositional phrase 'to the king' that marks the recipient; the whole clause means 'Now I shall compose a certain letter to the king.'
segments.s069-g98: This clause continues with the sending action: فرستم is first-person present 'I send / I will send'. The preposition به + دست ('into the hand') marks the means or bearer ('I send by/into the hand of...'), and تو–ای is a vocative address ('O you' / poetic 'thou'), followed by the epithet نیک‌خواه ('well-wisher'). The verb is placed before the addressee phrase for emphasis: 'I will send [it] into your hand, O well-wisher.'
segments.s069-g99: سخن هر چه باید به یاد آورم: 'سخن' (speech/words) is the theme; 'هر چه باید' is a relative phrase 'whatever ought (to be done/said)' modifying سخن. به یاد آوردن is a light-verb construction meaning 'to bring to memory / to recall / to relate', with آورم first-person present 'I bring/recall'. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect, but the clause means 'I will recall / relate every necessary word.'
segments.s069-g100: روان و دلش سوی داد آورم: 'روان و دل' = 'soul and heart', with the enclitic ش marking 'his' (دلش = his heart). سوی + noun gives direction 'toward', so سوی داد = 'toward justice'; آورم (first-person) 'I bring' completes the verb phrase. داد here is the abstract noun 'justice' (not the past tense 'gave'), so the clause means 'I will bring/turn his soul and heart toward justice.'
segments.s069-g101: اگر یار باشد جهاندار ما: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). یار ('ally/friend') here functions as a predicative noun; باشد is the third-person present/subjunctive of بودن ('be'), giving a protasis 'if ... be'. جهاندار ما ('our sovereign/world-lord') is the (inverted) subject phrase referring to who would be the ally; the inversion (predicate before subject) is a common poetic device. Literal reading: 'If our sovereign be (or becomes) an ally.'
segments.s069-g102: به کام تو گردد همه کار ما: به کام تو is a prepositional phrase 'to/according to your desire'; گردد is third-person present/subjunctive of گردیدن 'become/turn out' and expresses the resulting state ('may/shall turn out'); همه کار ما 'all our affairs' is the theme. The clause means 'all our affairs will turn out as you wish.'
segments.s069-g103: نویسنده را پیش بنشاندند: نویسنده (the scribe) takes the object marker را, indicating it is definite and the direct object; پیش here means 'before/in the presence (of them)'. بنشاندند is past tense, third-person plural 'they seated/placed (him)'. The clause literally: 'They seated the scribe before [them].'
segments.s069-g104: ز هر در سخنها همی راندند: ز هر در = 'from every door/quarter' (ز is the poetic preposition 'from'); سخنها 'the speeches/talks' is the object (plural). همی is a classical/progressive particle indicating continued or habitual action, and راندند is past plural 'they drove away'. Together: 'they drove away talk from every door,' i.e. they silenced or dispersed speakers everywhere.
segments.s069-g257: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. آن جامه‌ها is the noun phrase 'those garments' (آن determiner + plural جامه‌ها). سوده here qualifies جامه‌ها and appears as a descriptive element placed between the noun and the verb (in Ferdowsī's word-order the modifier can appear before the verb); بفگند is a classical narrative past (ب + فگند, archaic form of فکند) meaning 'threw/cast' and takes جامه‌ها as its object. Finally شب sits after the verb for a poetic pause: it resumes the frame-setting (”when … night …”).
segments.s069-g258: سپیده is the subject 'the dawn' and بخندید is a simple past (3sg) 'laughed'. The conjunction و links the following verb phrase: بگشاد is another past-form (ب + گشاد, archaic for گشود) meaning 'opened', and لب is its object ('lip' or idiomatically 'mouth' — لب گشودن = to open the mouth/speak). The clause thus narrates two successive actions performed by the dawn.
segments.s069-g259: بیامد is the past tense 'he came'. به زین is a prepositional phrase 'to the saddle' (به + زین). اندر آورد پای is a compact sequence meaning 'he put/placed his foot in' (اندر = 'into/in', آورد = past of آوردن 'brought/placed', پای = 'foot'); the verb آورد governs پای as its object. Word order places the motion verb (بیامد) first, then the locative (به زین) and the secondary action (اندر آورد پای).
segments.s069-g260: برآمد is an intransitive past 'arose/issued forth'. خروشیدن here is used as a verbal noun/infinitive ('the crying/roaring') and functions as the semantic subject of برآمد (i.e. 'the crying arose'). کره نای is a genitive phrase modifying خروشیدن — 'the cry of the nây (reed‑flute)'; in classical poetry the ezāfe is often omitted, so کره ('young/small/offspring' or intensifier) qualifies نای. Together the segment means 'the reed‑flute's cry rose.'
segments.s069-g261: به سوی is the prepositional phrase 'toward' (به + سوی). شهنشاه is the addressee 'the sovereign/king'. بنهاد is a past tense verb (ب + نهاد, classical form of نهادن) meaning 'set/laid/placed' and here combines with روی ('face') to mean 'he turned/set his face toward' — روی is the object/primary noun meaning 'face' (i.e. he directed his gaze toward the king).
segments.s069-g262: This noun group supplies what was offered to the king. نامه is 'letter' and سام is the proper name 'Sam' in genitive relation (Sam's letter). آزاده خوی is a compound epithet 'noble‑hearted' / 'noble of disposition' describing Sam (آزاده = noble, خوی = disposition/character). ابا is a classical word meaning 'reluctance/refusal' (or the verb 'to be reluctant'); here it appears in a compact poetic usage and most naturally reads as indicating the act of presenting the letter (the line omits explicit auxiliaries) — the whole phrase thus functions as '(he) presented the letter of Sam, the noble‑hearted.'
segments.s069-g113: به رزم is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in/at battle' (poetic use of به). اندرون 'inside/within' marks the internal locus. زهر تریاک سوز is a compact nominal phrase: زهر (head noun 'poison') is qualified by the following nouns تریاک 'opium' and سوز 'burning' used adjectivally — i.e., 'a poison that burns like opium' or 'the opium-burning poison'. The verb is omitted here (poetic verbless clause) and completed by the parallel clause.
segments.s069-g114: به بزم 'in the banquet/assembly' parallels به رزم. اندرون again marks 'within' (the person's inner self). ماه گیتی فروز is a noun phrase where ماه 'the moon' is followed by the verbal/adjectival فروز 'world-illuminating' (literally 'making the world bright'), so 'the inner moon that lights the world.' The clause supplies the sense/verb (فروز) that completes the contrast with the first half.
segments.s069-g115: گراینده گرز uses an agent noun گراینده 'one who directs' directly before the object گرز 'mace' without an explicit ezāfe (a compressed, poetic style): 'the one who guides the mace.' و links the coordinated phrase to گشاینده شهر, where گشاینده 'opener' modifies شهر 'city' (i.e. 'conqueror who opens the city').
segments.s069-g116: ز شادی is a poetic use of ز (از) meaning 'from' or 'through' indicating source/motive: 'from/through joy.' به هر کس 'to every person' is the indirect object. رساننده بهر uses the participial/agentive رساننده 'one who brings/delivers' and بهر ('bahr') here as 'portion/benefit' — so 'bringing to each his share/benefit.' The participle stands as an attributive/predicative description of the same subject.
segments.s069-g117: کشنده درفش فریدون به جنگ: کشنده here is used in the sense 'one who draws or bears' (pulling/tractive), taking درفش فریدون 'Fereydun's banner/standard' as its object; به جنگ 'to/into battle' indicates destination. This is a compressed, nominative phrase describing an agent (banner-bearer to battle).
segments.s069-g118: کشنده here shifts to the more usual meaning 'killer' or 'slayer' and functions as an appositive/epithet; سرافراز 'lofty/triumphant' is an adjective qualifying that epithet. جنگی پلنگ is a compound: جنگی 'warlike' modifies پلنگ 'panther' (a metaphorical epithet for a fierce warrior). The sequence is typical Persian apposition and adjective order in poetic compression.
segments.s069-g119: ز باد عمود تو is a genitive sequence 'from the wind/gust of your column/pillar' (ز = 'from' in poetic register). کوه بلند is the NP 'the mountain tall' but the verb is postponed to the next segment (common in epic poetry): literally 'from the gust of your column, the mountain [becomes] tall.'
segments.s069-g120: شود is the present/simple verb 'becomes' or 'happens' and supplies the action for the previous clause. خاک نعل سرافشان سمند is a noun phrase where خاک نعل 'the dust of the horseshoe' (noun+noun, ezāfe omitted) is qualified by سرافشان 'scattering' (participle/adjectival), and سمند 'steed' is the head noun placed at the end — 'the steed that scatters the horseshoe-dust.' The verb and this descriptive NP together complete the image.
segments.s069-g153: میان را ببستم is an action with the object میان (marked by را) placed before the verb; the verb ببستم is an archaic/past verbal form meaning 'I closed/took my stand' with an implicit first‑person subject. به نام بلند is a prepositional/agentive phrase 'with/under the lofty name' (بلند modifying نام) that comments on how the speaker took position; the whole phrase attaches adverbially to the verb that follows.
segments.s069-g154: نشستم is a first‑person past action 'I sat / I mounted' (here used of mounting a horse) with the locative phrase بران ('بر' + 'آن' contracted) meaning 'on that'. پیل پیکر is a compound modifier: پیل 'elephant' modifies پیکر 'body/shape' (so 'elephant‑bodied'), and سمند is the head noun 'steed'. Word order is verb then prepositional phrase + compound noun, typical of poetic inversion.
segments.s069-g155: به زین اندرون is a locative expression ('in/on the saddle’s inner part'); به is the preposition, زین 'saddle' is the locus and اندرون 'inside/within' further specifies location. گِرزه گاوسر is a noun phrase where گِرزه 'mace' is specified by the name گاوسر (a proper name or epithet of the mace).
segments.s069-g156: This is a list of armaments placed on the rider: به بازو کمان = 'on the arm, a bow' (به + locative noun بازو with کمان as the thing located there). و links the next item; به گردن سپر = 'and on the neck, a shield.' The construction uses repeated به + noun to mark the locations of the weapons.
segments.s069-g157: برفتم here is a past tense verb meaning 'I rose / I went forth' (the provided senses do not match the usage, so the verb sense is not among the listed senses). بسان 'like' introduces the simile, followed by نهنگ 'whale' and the adjective دژم 'grim/sullen' describing that image.
segments.s069-g158: This is an existential/possessive elliptic construction common in Persian: مرا تیز چنگ literally 'to me a sharp claw' meaning 'I had/there was to me a sharp claw'; ورا (= بر + آن contracted) refers back to the creature just mentioned, 'to it a sharp tail' (تیز modifying both چنگ and دم). The object marker را is attached to emphasize the noun phrase; و links the two possessed items.
segments.s069-g159: This clause means 'whoever heard (it) gave me a farewell.' مرا is the object 'me' (marked by را); کرد here is the past tense 'made/did' used with پدرود 'farewell' to mean 'gave a farewell' or 'bade farewell'; هرکو شنید is a relative/conditional phrase 'whoever heard.' Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s069-g160: که introduces the reported content 'that …'. بر اژدها uses بر with the sense 'against' to mark the target (the dragon), گرز is the weapon 'mace', and خواهم is the auxiliary indicating future ('I will'); کشید (here an archaic/fixed form) is the main verb 'to strike/draw/wield'—the provided senses for کشید do not match this verbal use and are therefore not applicable.
segments.s069-g105: سرنامه کرد is verb + object in the simple past: سرنامه (a prefatory heading) is the direct object and کرد is the 3rd-person singular past 'made/did'. آفرین خدای is a noun phrase meaning the acclamation 'Praise (to) God'; the expected ezafe between آفرین and خدای is omitted in poetic/archaic style, so the phrase reads as 'the acclamation (praise) of God'.
segments.s069-g106: کجا is an interrogative 'where'. هست و باشد pairs the indicative هست ('is') with باشد used in an optative/subjunctive sense ('may be' / 'may remain'), a common poetic device to state and wish simultaneously. همیشه به جای is a prepositional phrase 'always in place' (به here functioning as the preposition used with جای). The subject (God) is understood from the previous clause.
segments.s069-g107: ازویست is a contracted poetic form of از وی است ('it is from Him'); it predicates the following coordinated items. نیک و بد و هست و نیست lists opposites ('good and bad, being and non-being') as the things that originate from Him; the verb (است) is fused into ازویست so the list is the compound nominal complement.
segments.s069-g108: همه بندگانیم is a nominal sentence with the plural copula -یم ('we are') attached to بندگان ('servants'); syntactically it reads 'all (are) servants' or 'we are all servants' in a generalizing sense. و links this to the following clause, ایزد یکیست, where یکیست is a 3rd-person predicate 'is one' asserting God's oneness.
segments.s069-g109: هر آن چیز کو ساخت اندر بوش contains several archaic features: کو is the older relative pronoun equivalent to که ('which/that') rather than the interrogative 'where'; ساخت is the past 'made/fashioned'; اندر = 'in/within'. بوش is an archaic contracted form meaning 'in him / of him' (etymologically related to بویش or به او) and functions as a genitive/location complement here. The whole segment reads 'every thing which he made within him'.
segments.s069-g110: بران است is a locative/particle + copula: بران (بر آن) = 'on/thereon' followed by است 'is'. The subject that follows, چرخ, here denotes the celestial wheel or fate (چرخ as cosmic wheel/fate), and روان را روش literally 'the soul (روان) را (object/topic marker) [its] way' — روش is 'way/manner'. The construction uses را to mark روان as the affected entity: 'the charkh is the way for the soul' (word order inverted for poetic effect).
segments.s069-g111: خداوند introduces a sovereign or lord (here governing celestial bodies) followed by a coordinated list of celestial names (کیوان، خورشید، ماه). In this syntactic position خداوند functions as a title or epithet 'lord/master' that scopes over the enumerated heavenly bodies.
segments.s069-g112: وزو is an archaic/contracted form (likely وز او / از او) meaning roughly 'and from them' or 'and likewise from him' and does not match the modern sense list provided, so its sense is flagged as uncertain. آفرین here functions as a noun/exclamation used as 'blessing/praise' and بر is the preposition 'upon', so آفرین بر منوچهر شاه reads 'a blessing/praise upon Manuchehr the king' (a conventional benediction).
segments.s069-g49: This is an initial interrogative/locative phrase. کجا (where) opens the question; دیزه تو is a possessive noun phrase (دیزه 'fortress' + تو 'your'), with the possessor after the possessed as in older poetic order. چمد (here 'bolt, latch') names the particular part of the fortress. روز جنگ ('the day of battle') is a temporal adjunct that sets the scene for the coming verb in the next segment. Note there is no verb inside this segment — it is a fronted noun phrase and adverbial temporal phrase.
segments.s069-g50: شتاب آید is a verbal phrase where شتاب (a noun 'haste') is the subject/nominal that 'comes' (آید, present/subjunctive 3rd sg of آمدن used here as simple present/occurrent). اندر سپاه is a locative/prepositional phrase ('within/in the host/army') and درنگ is a nominal predicate 'pause/delay' placed after the locative; Persian allows such verbless nominal predication (i.e. '(there is) pause'). The overall sense is causal/temporal: when haste comes on battle-day, there is (no) tarrying in the army (the negative is implied by context of urgency).
segments.s069-g51: سپهری is an adjective/relational adverb 'heavenly, of the sky' or 'in the heavens'; کجا باد is a formulaic optative/rhetorical 'where / how should it be' with باد functioning as an optative/modal particle ('may it be' / 'would that'); گرز تو دید is a verbal clause 'saw/beheld your mace' (دید here is past tense 'beheld'). Together the segment forms a hypothetical/rhetorical clause about whether the heavens could behold the hero's mace.
segments.s069-g52: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily/surely' introducing the consequence clause. ستاره is 'star' (the celestial bodies). The two final words (نیارد کشید) are irregular/archaic here and their exact morphology in this line is obscure: نیارد does not match the provided lexicon senses and appears to be a contracted/older form (possibly a negative or modal form of آوردن) and کشید (from کشیدن) would literally mean 'drew/dragged/struck'. Because these items are irregular or corrupt in the supplied lemma list their precise grammatical function is uncertain; contextually they express that the star would not endure/ would be overcome. (Senses for these two tokens are therefore not assigned from the provided list.)
segments.s069-g53: زمین is the subject 'the earth/land'; نسپرد is the past form of سپردن 'to entrust/hand over' (3rd person past), so 'the earth entrusted'; شیر is the direct object 'the lion' (no overt را here, typical in older poetic Persian); با داد تو is an instrumental/prepositional phrase 'by/with your justice' modifying how the entrusting was done. The clause follows a classical S–O–V order: subject + verb + object + adjunct.
segments.s069-g54: This segment lists two nouns روان و خرد ('soul/spirit and wisdom') followed by کشته which here qualifies them as 'of the slain' (a participial/attributive use). بنیاد is used in the sense 'to found/establish' (in classical Persian a noun can appear in such predicative/agentive uses: 'you are the founder' or 'you founded'); تو is the possessive/agent 'you'. The most natural reading is that the subject ('you') is credited with establishing or providing for the souls and wisdom of the slain — i.e. 'you established the souls and wisdom of the slain.'
segments.s069-g55: همه مردم is a simple noun phrase 'all the people/everyone'. از داد تو is a causal/prepositional phrase 'because of/through your justice' (از used poetically), and شادمان is a predicate adjective 'joyful/happy'. This is a verbless clause where the adjective predicates the subject (common in Persian: 'the people are joyful').
segments.s069-g56: ز تو is an ablative/agent phrase 'from/through you'; داد یابد is a verbal phrase where یابد (present/3rd sg of یافتن 'to find/attain') takes داد 'justice' as its object — 'finds/attains justice'. زمین و زمان is a coordinated object (literally 'earth and time' = 'the world and the ages'). The clause thus says that by/through you earth and time obtain justice.
segments.s069-g161: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from' (old/poetic form of از). 'سر' means the creature's head. 'تا' (here 'from ... تا') marks the extent 'as far as / up to', and 'به دمش' = 'to/unto its دم' (archaic use: دم = tail). 'چو کوه بلند' is a simile: 'chū' = 'like/as', with 'کوه بلند' = 'a high mountain' as the predicate describing the length/height.
segments.s069-g162: کشان is a present/adjectival participle meaning 'dragging, pulling' that modifies what follows. 'موی سر' = 'the hair of the head' (موی = hair, سر = head). 'بر زمین' = 'upon the ground' with بر as the locative/on preposition. 'چون کمند' is another simile: 'like a noose/lasso', so the clause says the hair is pulled on the ground as if it were a lasso.
segments.s069-g163: زبانش is 'its tongue' (زبان + possessive ش). 'بسان' = 'like/as' (synonym of چو), and 'درختی سیاه' is a simile 'a black tree' describing the tongue's appearance or shape.
segments.s069-g164: This clause uses the poetic preposition ز = 'from' with فر (here taken as 'radiance/glory') followed by the compound verbal idea 'باز کرده فگنده' — 'باز کرده' = 'has opened' (past participle/compound verb), and فگنده (فگَنده) = 'thrown/spread' (archaic past participle). Together they mean it opened and cast/spread something 'ز فر' (from its radiance) 'به راه' = 'onto the road/way'. The order is poetic; English idiom is rendered more directly.
segments.s069-g165: چو = 'when'. آبگیرش literally 'his water-pools' (آبگیر) is a poetic image for the eyes; the possessive ش marks 'his'. 'پر از خون' = 'full of blood' with پر = 'full (of)' + از = 'of'. The final 'دو چشم' appositive restates that the pools are the two eyes.
segments.s069-g166: This is a sequence of past simple verbs. 'مرا' (here tokenized as م + را) is the accusative 'me' (definite object), 'دید' = 'saw' (past). 'غرید' = 'roared' (past), and 'و آمد به خشم' = 'and came in anger' where آمد is past 'came' and 'به خشم' = 'into anger'. Word order is subject-verb omitted (the creature is subject), then verb sequence describing its reaction.
segments.s069-g167: گمانی = 'a supposition/guess'; چنان = 'such'. بردم is the 1st-person past of بردن 'I took/held' and here means 'I entertained/assumed (a thought)'. 'ای شهریار' is a direct address 'O king' (vocative).
segments.s069-g168: که introduces the content/result clause 'that ...'. دارم is present (or historic-present sense here) 'I have/hold'. مگر in this context is a rhetorical/as-if particle ('as if' / 'as though'); the phrase 'مگر آتش اندر کنار' = 'as if (there were) fire at my side.' اندر = poetic/informal 'in/within' and کنار = 'side/beside'.
segments.s069-g177: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning "when." شد here functions as an auxiliary forming a passive/state ('was sewn'). دوخته is the past participle "sewn." کران means "edge/corner," and از دهانش (literally "from his mouth") marks the source; word order (auxiliary + participle + noun phrase) is a poetic/classical sequence meaning "when one corner of his mouth became sewn."
segments.s069-g178: بماند is used narratively as a past-state verb "remained/was left." از شگفتی here means "because of/from astonishment" (giving the reason). به بیرون is an adverbial phrase "to the outside/outward," and زبانِ ش (zabān‑eš) is "his tongue." Together: "his tongue remained out from astonishment."
segments.s069-g179: هم اندر زمان دیگری همچنان is an adverbial temporal frame: هم "also/again," اندر زمان دیگری "at another time," and همچنان "in the same way/likewise." The whole phrase sets the scene for a repeated action.
segments.s069-g180: زدم is 1st‑person past "I struck." بر introduces the target "on/at his mouth" (دهانش). بپیچید is the past of پیچیدن with the b‑prefix, here intransitive "twisted/turned," and ازان "from that" gives the cause/source. The subject of بپیچید is implied (the tongue or the thing struck), so the clause reads "I struck his mouth; from that it twisted."
segments.s069-g181: سدیگر (classical/poetic) functions adverbially as "again/another (time)" here; زدم is past "I struck." بر میان means "upon the middle/midsection." زفرش (a poetic noun) is tied to the place struck (a chest/upper‑belly area) in this passage. The whole segment means "I struck again at his midsection."
segments.s069-g182: برآمد is a past intransitive "arose/issued forth." همی is an emphatic/narrative particle here ("forthwith/just then"). جوی خون is a noun phrase "a stream of blood," and از جگرش specifies the source "from his liver." The clause orderly expresses the result of the blow.
segments.s069-g183: چو is the temporal "when." تنگ اندر آورد is an idiomatic causative phrase "to press (someone) tightly/close" (آورد as "bring/cause" with تنگ "tight/close"). با من marks the person affected ("me"), and زمین is the goal/place "to the ground/earth." So the segment means "when he pressed me close to the ground."
segments.s069-g184: برآهختم is first‑person past meaning "I sighed aloud/I lamented." این گاوسر گرز کین is a nominal phrase—این "this," گاوسر used as a proper name or a named object, گرز "mace/club," and کین "of vengeance/vengeful." The speaker utters or laments these words after being pressed down.
segments.s069-g121: همان is a demonstrative 'that' (here pointing back to the person just described). از + noun (از دل‌پاک و پاکیزه کیش) marks origin or quality: 'from/of a pure‑hearted and clean character.' دل‌پاک and پاکیزه are attributive adjectives describing کیش ('character/temper'), with the coordinating و linking the two adjectives.
segments.s069-g122: به آبشخور gives the directional phrase 'to/at the watering‑place.' آری here functions as an emphatic/insertive adverb 'indeed,' and همی is the old habitual/continuous particle 'always/still' placed before the nominal phrase that follows; the line omits an explicit verb, so the clause reads as a compressed poetic statement 'indeed always (to the water‑hole) wolf and ewe' (i.e. brings/come).
segments.s069-g123: یکی bendeh‑style opening 'a certain / one' + بنده‌ام is a humble self‑reference ('I am a servant'); the sequence بنده‌ام من places the pronominal من for emphasis, and رسیده به جای is a perfect/past participle construction 'have arrived/reached to a place' (رسیده = arrived, به جای = to a place). Word order is emphatic: the appositive 'one, my humble self' precedes the verbal phrase.
segments.s069-g124: This clause is built around the past participle آورده ('brought') used with پای as its object: 'brought (one's) foot.' The two prepositional phrases (به مردی 'to a man' and به شست اندر 'into the thumb/palm') show direction/goal — 'to a man, into his palm/hand' — so the meaning is 'placed/put my foot into a man's hand' (an idiom of submission). Note that شست here is used in a classical/poetic spatial sense (see senses); اندر is the classical preposition 'into/within.'
segments.s069-g125: همی is the habitual adverb 'continually/ever.' گرد کافور is a noun phrase 'the dust/powder of camphor' (گرد here = 'dust/powder'), placed before the verb گیرد ('takes/seizes'). The verb گیرد is a present/ongoing third‑person form meaning 'seizes/takes,' and سرم is the possessive form 'my head' (object whose state is described) — the line therefore reads 'camphor‑dust continually seizes my head' (i.e. my head is always perfumed/covered with camphor).
segments.s069-g126: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying کرد (simple past 'did/made'). خورشید و ماه is the compound subject 'the sun and the moon,' and افسرم is the direct object 'my helmet' (افسر + possessive -م). Word order is Subject–Object–Verb inverted for poetry: effectively 'thus the sun and moon made my helmet.'
segments.s069-g127: ببستم is a perfect/past verb 'I closed/tied' (here used transitively), with میان را the definite object 'the middle / the space between' marked by the object marker را. یکی بنده‌وار is an adverbial/appositive phrase 'in a servile/humble manner' ('like a humble servant') describing how the action was done; note the poetic inversion that places the manner phrase after the object for emphasis.
segments.s069-g128: ساختم is the first‑person past 'I built/made' with کارزار as its object 'battle/field/array.' جاودان is an adjective 'eternal/everlasting' qualifying کارزار; the initial word ابا is archaic/poetic and uncertain here (the listed senses give 'refusal/reluctance'), so its exact force is unclear in modern terms — the clause reads 'I built an everlasting battle/array,' with ابا functioning as a stylistic particle.
segments.s069-g129: This is a pile-up of descriptive compounds and adjectives that characterize the (implied) rider. Persian places modifiers after or beside the nouns they modify in poetry; عنان پیـچ (literally 'reins-twist') is a compound describing someone skilled with the reins, اسپ افگن is an adjectival compound 'horse‑glittering' (افگن = 'making shine'), and گرزدار is an adjective meaning 'mace‑bearing'. The coordinating و simply links the items in the string of epithets.
segments.s069-g130: چو here functions as a simile/comparison ('like'/'as'); the clause is inverted in typical classical Persian order: 'چو من کس ندیدی به گیتی سوار' = 'You have seen no person like me, a rider, in the world.' ندیدی is 2nd‑person past (you did not see), and به گیتی inserts the locative 'in the world.' The whole clause addresses the addressee: no rider in the world like me have you seen.
segments.s069-g131: بشد is the past of شدن and here means 'it happened/it was' or 'it turned/was put to flight'; آب گردان is a poetic compound (literally 'water‑troop' or 'revolving waters') used as a martial image for a hostile force or flood, and مازندران names the region. Together they form the subject that undergoes the action.
segments.s069-g132: چو here means 'when' (temporal). دست بردم is an idiom 'I laid/put my hand' or 'I took up' (بردم = I took/raised), followed by به + گرز گران 'to/onto the heavy mace' (گران = 'heavy/weighty'). The clause gives the time/condition for the turning expressed in the previous segment.
segments.s069-g133: ز ... گر is a classical conditional set: ز من‌گر نبودی = 'if it were not for me' (ز here introduces the causal/conditional notion). نبودی is the past/conditional negative of بودن ('were not'), and به گیتی نشان = 'a sign/trace in the world' is the consequence clause's object.
segments.s069-g134: برآورده is used here in a passive/resultive sense ('would have been lifted/borne away'); گردن is the direct object 'necks/heads'. ز گردن کشان marks the agent/source with ز + an agentive form ('from/by the neck‑pullers' — those who pull necks = violent attackers). The phrase conveys the hypothetical violent result had the speaker not acted.
segments.s069-g135: چنان ... کو functions as a relative clause 'such a ... that/which': چنان اژدها کو ... = 'such a dragon that ...'. کو is an archaic/poetic relative (equivalent to modern که). ز رود کشف literally 'uncovered from the river' (ز = 'from', کشف = 'uncovering/uncoverly emergence'), so the clause describes the dragon's emergence from the river.
segments.s069-g136: برون آمد is a simple past intransitive 'came out / emerged'. و کرد here is the light verb 'and made/caused' (کرد + object), and گیتی چو کف is a simile 'the world like foam' (چو = 'like', کف = 'foam'), i.e. the dragon's coming made the world appear foamy/chaotic. The sequence is straightforward past narration.
segments.s069-g225: This clause is an indefinite subject + predicate: 'یکی' functions as 'someone', 'آرزو دارد' is present-tense possession ('has a wish' / 'wishes') with 'دارد' as the present auxiliary/possessive verb. 'اندر نهان' is an adverbial prepositional phrase ('in secret' / 'secretly') placed after the predicate for emphasis.
segments.s069-g226: 'بیاید' is a 3rd-person subjunctive/optative form ('may come'), and 'بخواهد' is 3rd-person present of 'خواستن' used to mark desire ('wishes' / 'would want'). In classical/poetic Persian the subordinate clause expressing the wished-for event can contain two modal verbs in sequence; 'ز شاه جهان' supplies the referent ('the king of the world') associated with that subordinate clause. Word order is somewhat inverted (modal verbs precede the agent phrase) as is common in poetry.
segments.s069-g227: 'یکی' again means 'someone'. 'آرزو' + 'دارد' (implicit from prior clause) expresses 'hopes/wishes for'. 'کان' here is an archaic/poetic connective meaning roughly 'that which' or 'what', so 'آرزو کان' = 'wishes for that which'. 'به یزدان نکوست' literally 'is good with God' (i.e. pleasing to God); 'به' is the preposition 'to/with' and 'یزدان' = God.
segments.s069-g228: 'کجا' here introduces a reason/explanatory clause ('for/since/where'), not a question. 'نیکویی' is a noun 'goodness/virtue'. 'زیر' = 'under' (here 'subject to/under the control of') and governs 'فرمان اوست' ('is under his command'). 'ست' is the copula 'is' (classical form: ست = است).
segments.s069-g229: 'نکردیم' is the simple past negative, 'we did not do/act'. 'بی‌رای' is a compound prepositional/adjectival phrase meaning 'without (the) counsel/opinion/consent'. It modifies the verb phrase and is followed by 'شاه بزرگ' ('the great king'), the authority whose counsel was not omitted.
segments.s069-g230: This clause gives the reason: the conjunction 'که' introduces it ('for/that'). 'بنده' = 'servant' (humble self or literal servant). 'نباید' = 'must not / should not' (impersonal obligation). The second 'که' is a subordinator, and 'باشد' is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive of 'to be' ('be / should be'). 'سترگ' is used figuratively here ('proud / haughty'); literally it relates to the eye/sight (so the phrase conveys 'a servant should not lift/raise his eyes' — i.e. be proud).
segments.s069-g231: 'همانا' is an emphatic particle meaning 'indeed' or 'verily'. 'که' introduces the clause. 'با زال' = 'with Zāl' (the legendary hero), and 'پیمان من' = 'my pact/covenant' (possessive 'من' = my). Word order places the prepositional phrase and object before the verb in this poetic line.
segments.s069-g232: 'شنیدست' is an archaic/perfect form meaning 'has heard' (3rd person). It heads the predicate; what follows, 'شاه جهان‌بان من', is an appositive/title: 'my king, the world's guardian' (شاه = king, جهان‌بان = world‑guardian/protector, من = my). The sequence means 'my king, the world's guardian, has heard (it)'.
segments.s069-g145: نهنگ دژم is a noun + adjective (noun first, adjective after) meaning “grim whale.” برکشیدی is the verb: the prefix بر- (indicating upward motion) plus کشیدی (2nd-person past “you pulled/raised”), so the clause reads “you raised ... up.” ز آب is a postverbal prepositional phrase “from the water.”
segments.s069-g146: به دم is an instrumental phrase here (“by the tail/edge”); درکشیدی is again past 2nd-person with the prefix در- (here meaning draw down/drag), so “you drew (down).” ز گردون عقاب places the source and the noun after it: “an eagle from the sky.” The whole clause: “by the tail you drew an eagle from the sky.”
segments.s069-g147: زمین گشت uses گشت (past/preterite of گشتن) with زمین as subject: “the land became.” بی‌مردم and then و چارپای are predicates/nominal complements meaning “devoid of people and (even) of four‑footed animals,” listed after the verb in typical poetic order.
segments.s069-g148: همه (subject) + یکسر (adverb, “entirely/utterly”) modify سپردند (past 3rd‑pl. “they surrendered/handed over”). او را is the object with the definite object marker را (“to him”), and جای is the thing given/surrendered (“place”); literal order follows older Persian syntax: “All utterly handed him the place.”
segments.s069-g149: چو دیدم (“when I saw”) is a temporal clause head (چو = when). که introduces the subordinate clause; اندر جهان is a locative prepositional phrase “in the world.” کس نبود is the predicate “there was no one” (کس here = no one).
segments.s069-g150: که + relative clause: که با او (“who with him”) همی (adverbial emphatic/continuous) modifies the phrase that follows. دست here means “help/hand” and یارست is predicative (“is/acts as a friend/ally”), while سود (“benefit”) is the evaluative noun—together the older phrasing means “who, as a helping hand/ally with him, would be of benefit.”
segments.s069-g151: به زور is an instrumental phrase “by the might/strength.” جهاندار یزدان پاک is a string of epithets in apposition: “world‑holder (جهاندار), God (یزدان), pure (پاک)” — typical poetic stacking of divine titles before the verb.
segments.s069-g152: بیفگندم is an optative/subjunctive older form meaning roughly “may he cast” or “may it be cast” (بی- prefix + افگندم), here a prayerful wish addressed to God. از دل همه ترس و باک is the object phrase “from the heart(s) all fear and dread” (از = from; دل = heart; همه = all; ترس و باک = fear and dread).
segments.s069-g249: گسی is an indefinite pronoun here meaning “a certain person / someone.” کردمش is the past stem کرد plus enclitic object/tense markers (poetic contracted form) meaning “sent/caused/put him (forth).” با + دلی مستمند is an instrumental/attendant phrase “with a needy heart” where دلی is attributive (“heartfelt”) and مستمند is an adjective ‘needy/poor’; the whole segment says that he dispatched/let the person go while possessing a needy/afflicted heart.
segments.s069-g250: چو is the subordinating particle “when.” آید is the present/epistolary form of آمدن used in narratives to mean “comes / approaches.” به نزدیک combines the preposition به + adjective/nominal نزدیک to mean “to/near,” and تخت بلند is the noun تخت “throne” modified postpositively by بلـند “high/lofty.” The clause means “when he comes near the high throne.”
segments.s069-g251: همان کن is an imperative: همان “the same / that same” + کن “do (imperative).” که introduces the comparison/subordinate clause “as / when.” با مهتری literally “with a superior/elder” (مهتری = preeminence / elder) and در خورد uses خورد in the sense “to eat” (i.e., “at table / in company”): together the phrase advises behaving as one would when dining with a superior.
segments.s069-g252: ترا is the object/pronominal form “to you,” را is the definite/object marker (here emphasizing the person), and خود is emphatic “yourself.” نیاموخت is the negative past of آموختن (“did not learn”); the negative prefix ن‑ attaches to give “did not learn.” باید is the modal “must / it is necessary,” and خرد is the noun “wisdom.” The sense: “You yourself were not taught (or did not learn); you must (therefore seek) wisdom.”
segments.s069-g253: چو “when.” نامه نوشتند is a past plural clause “they wrote the letter” (نوشتند = they wrote). و شد is the connective “and it came to be / and became,” and رای راست is “a right/true plan or counsel” (رای = counsel/decision, راست = right/firm). Put together: “When they wrote the letter and the counsel was settled.”
segments.s069-g254: ستد is the past of ستود/ستادن in the sense used here “seized / took hold of.” زود is an adverb “quickly/soon.” دستان (lit. “hands”) is the object of ستد (he seized [their] hands). و connects the clauses, and بر پای خاست is a fixed phrase “rose to his feet” (بر = onto/upon, پای = foot, خاست = arose).
segments.s069-g255: چو “when.” خورشید is “the sun.” سر سوی خاور نهاد is a perf. construction: سر “head,” سوی “toward,” خاور “east,” and نهاد (past of نهادن) “placed/put” used idiomatically as “turned.” So the clause means “when the sun turned its head toward the east.”
segments.s069-g256: نخفت is the negative past of خفتن “did not sleep.” و joins the next verb نیاسود, literally “and did not rest/was not comforted.” تا is the temporal preposition “until,” and بامداد means “dawn / morning.” The segment: “he neither slept nor rested until dawn.”
segments.s069-g201: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. ز + و here function together as the old contracted prepositional phrase 'from him' (ز = from; و is the clitic standing for 'him/it' in this position). بازگشتم is simple past 1st-person 'I returned' (subject pronoun omitted because the verb marks person). تن روشنم is the noun + adjective + possessive structure ('my bright/shining body'), with -م attached to روشن marking 'my'. Word order is the expected classical Persian: temporal marker + prepositional phrase + verb, followed by the noun phrase.
segments.s069-g202: برهنه شد is a past-tense resultative: 'became naked/was laid bare'. از introduces the thing of which it was deprived ('of' / 'from'). نامور جوشنم is a genitive/possessive sequence: نامور 'renowned' modifying جوشن 'cuirass/armor', with -م on جوشن marking 'my'. The whole phrase thus says the body became bare of my famous armor.
segments.s069-g203: فرو ریخت is a compound verbal idea meaning 'fell down / tumbled down' (فرو = down(ward), ریخت = fell/poured); the verb is past in sense. از باره برگستوان is a prepositional phrase 'from the burden on (the) shoulder(s)' where باره = 'burden/weight' and برگستوان is a poetic compound meaning 'on the shoulder(s)' (بر + گستوان). The phrase explains from what/where it fell.
segments.s069-g204: وزین هست is a simple copular statement 'it is heavy/weighty' (وزین = heavy; هست = is). هر چند here is a concessive phrase 'though / however much' (هر = 'every/each' used with چند to form 'however (much/many)'). رانم is a possessive construction (ران + -م) meaning 'my thigh/flank' (poetic anatomy); زیان is the noun 'harm/detriment'. The clause therefore means that, although the object is heavy, it did harm to the speaker's thigh.
segments.s069-g205: بران بوم is a locative phrase 'on that land / upon that homeland' (بران = 'on/thereon', بوم = 'land, homeland'). تا سالیان is a duration expression 'for years'. بر نبود is the past negative copula 'there was not' (بر here stresses location, نبود = 'was not'). The sequence states that, for a long time, that land held nothing.
segments.s069-g206: جز از marks an exception: 'except (for)'. سوخته خار خاور is a noun phrase 'the scorched/burned thorn(s) of the east' (سوخته 'burned' modifying خار 'thorn', and خاور 'east' specifying origin). نبود is again the past negative 'there was not' used to close the clause; the whole segment says nothing remained there except the scorched eastern thorns.
segments.s069-g207: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. و جزین = 'and aside from this' (جزین = 'except/aside from this'), setting apart the following qualification. هر چه بودیم is a relative/comparative clause 'whatever we were' or 'whatever we had been' (هر چه = 'whatever', بودیم = 'we were' past). رای is the noun 'counsel/plan' and here picks up the idea of the preceding clause ('whatever counsel we had'). The whole phrase is a poetic fronting that prepares the object of the next clause.
segments.s069-g208: سران را is the direct object with the definite/object marker را: 'the chiefs/leaders (as object)'. سرآوردمی is a 1st-person past form meaning 'I brought (forth)/I made come' — here idiomatically 'I brought [their] heads' or 'I humbled/overturned them'. زیر پای is a locative phrase 'under the feet/underfoot', indicating the manner of domination. Together: 'I put the leaders under my feet.'
segments.s069-g241: چنان is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' modifying the whole clause; ماه is the subject ('the moon' or poetically 'the beautiful one'); بیند is the classical third‑person singular present of دیدن, here best read as 'is seen/appears'; به کابلستان is a locative phrase ('in/at Kabulistan') following the verb. Word order is classical/poetic (adverb — subject — verb — locative).
segments.s069-g242: چو introduces a simile 'like/as'; سرو سهی is a noun modified by an epithet (سۀی = 'dark/black' in a poetic sense), so 'a dark cypress'; بر سرش is a prepositional phrase 'upon its head' where ش is the enclitic possessive 'its'; گلستان is the noun 'rose‑garden'. The clause is elliptical (no verb) and functions as the simile completing 'چنان...'.
segments.s069-g243: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; دیوانه is predicate/adjective 'mad'; گردد is the classical present/subjunctive of گشتن meaning 'becomes'; نباشد شگفت is a copular negation 'there is no wonder' (negated بودن + noun), so the whole segment reads 'when he becomes mad, it is not surprising.' The negative copula follows the verb in this poetic order.
segments.s069-g244: ازو here means 'with/regarding him' or 'on account of him'; شاه را uses را as a topical/definite marker associated with 'the king' (the agent/affected person in the clause); کین is 'vengeance'; نباید is the impersonal modal 'should/must not'; گرفت is the infinitival/past form of گرفتن 'take'. The literal order 'ازو شاه را کین نباید گرفت' reads in English 'against him the king should not take vengeance' — i.e. 'the king should not take vengeance on him.'
segments.s069-g245: کنون is the adverb 'now'; رنج مهرش is a genitive phrase 'the anguish/suffering of his love' (مهرش = 'his love' with possessive -ش); به جایی رسید is an idiomatic perfective/past phrase 'reached a point' or 'came to such a state' (به + noun + رسید 'reached').
segments.s069-g246: که introduces a result/consequence clause 'such that'; بخشایش is the abstract noun 'forgiveness/mercy/generosity'; آرد is the classical third‑person singular present of آوردن 'brings/produces' (poetic form); هر آن کش بدید is an archaic relative phrase 'whichever person who saw' where کش functions as an agentive/nominal element ('one who...') and بدید is the past 'saw' (relative 'who saw'). The clause order is poetic (verb before its relative), giving 'that mercy it brought to whoever saw [him].'
segments.s069-g247: ز بس (ز = 'from/because of', بس = 'so much') forms the causal phrase 'because of so much'; درد is 'pain/suffering'; کو is an archaic relative/particle meaning roughly 'that/which' here linking to دید; دید is past 'he saw'; بر بی‌گناه is a prepositional phrase 'upon the innocent' (بر = 'on/upon', بی‌گناه = 'innocent'). Together: 'because he saw so much suffering upon the innocent...'.
segments.s069-g248: چنان is 'thus/so'; رفت is past of رفتن 'went' but in the idiom 'پیمان رفت' functions to indicate that a covenant/pledge was made or established (poetic usage); پیمان is 'covenant/pledge'; که introduces the following clause 'that/which'; بشنید is past 'heard' (classical form); شاه is 'the king'. The phrase means 'he made such a covenant as the king heard.' Note the inverted/poetic word order (verb and noun positions are flexible).
segments.s069-g81: که begins a subordinate clause ('that' / 'that you...'). ویران کنی is a verb phrase with the verb-root ویران (to ruin) plus the present/subjunctive/2nd-person ending کنی (‘you do/you destroy’) — verb at the end as in Persian. خان آباد من is a noun phrase 'khan (inn/estate) آباد (prosperous/inhabited) my' with the possessive pronoun من placed after the noun in poetic word order.
segments.s069-g82: چنین داد خواهی: چنین ('thus/such') + داد as the noun 'justice' or 'retribution', and خواهی is the 2nd-person form ('you will/wish/ask for'). The clause that follows is emphatic: همی is an emphatic/progressive particle ('indeed/ever') placed before the verb, and داد here is used as the verb 'give' with the postposed subject من ('I') for emphasis (poetic order). The whole expresses 'If you ask for such justice, I will indeed give it.'
segments.s069-g83: Typical Persian word order: subject pronoun من ('I') + temporal adverb اینک ('now'), then the prepositional phrase به پیش تو ('before you / in your presence'), and the verb استاده‌ام (poetic spelling of ایستاده‌ام) 'I am standing' / 'I have stood' with the 1st-person suffix -ام. The verb comes at the end of the clause.
segments.s069-g84: تن بنده is appositional/humble phrasing ('the body / I, your servant' — بنده used as a humble self-reference). خشمِـتـَـ (here shown as خشم + the 2nd-person clitic ت) + را marks 'your anger' as the definite/affected object (را). داده‌ام is perfect tense 'I have given/caused' (1st-person), so the clause literally says 'I have given (caused) your anger' — i.e. 'I have angered you.'
segments.s069-g85: Imperative construction: به اَرِّه ('with a saw') marks the instrument, میانم (میان + ـم) 'my middle' — i.e. 'me in the middle / my middle' — followed by بدو ('to it/him' or 'with it', here referring back to the saw) and نیم کن ('make half' = 'cut in two') with کن as the imperative. The clause orders instrument + object + imperative, typical in poetry.
segments.s069-g86: ز کابل مپیمای is a negative present/imperative: ز کابل 'from Kabul' + مپیمای (m‑ prefix + پیمای(ی) stem) — in classical Persian the prefix مـ can form a negative ('do not traverse/go'); so 'do not go away from Kabul'. با من سخن is an elliptical imperative meaning 'speak with me' (the auxiliary 'کن' is omitted, common in verse). Word order puts the source 'ز کابل' first, then the verb, then the imperative address.
segments.s069-g87: سپهبد is the subject ('the spahbed / general'). چو here means 'when' and introduces a temporal clause; بشنید is simple past 'he heard'. گفتار زال is the object 'Zal's speech/words' (ز marks the genitive 'of Zal'). The clause reads 'When the spahbed heard Zal's speech...'.
segments.s069-g88: برافراخت گوش is an idiomatic perfective sequence: برافراخت 'he raised/hoisted' + گوش 'ear' (here 'attention'/'he pricked up his ears'). و is the conjunction 'and'. فرو is an adverb 'down'/'inward', and برد is the past verb 'took/put' (here 'he drew/put down/inward'), with یال 'mane' as object — thus 'he pricked up his ears and drew in/lowered his mane.'
segments.s069-g209: "کجا" is an interrogative adverb ('where' / 'how'), placed at the start to mark a rhetorical question; "من" is the explicit first-person subject. "چمانیدمی" is an archaic past-form meaning 'to resemble / to be like' (here expressing 'could I have been like'), an old morphological shape equivalent to a past or potential past; it links to "بادپای" (the adjective/noun phrase 'swift-footed') as the complement denoting whom he would (not) resemble.
segments.s069-g210: "بپرداختی" is a past second-person verb ('you attended to / you set / you put'), here meaning 'you placed/put in position.' "شیر درنده" is a noun phrase with a head noun and adjective ('the fierce lion'), and "جای" is 'place/stead'; the whole segment says that the addressee put the fierce lion in that place (against which the speaker could not be 'swift-footed').
segments.s069-g211: "کنون" is an adverb 'now.' "چند سالست" is an idiomatic temporal expression meaning 'it is several years' (i.e. 'it has been many years'); here it introduces a duration. "تا پشت زین" means 'since / up to (the) saddle's back' i.e. 'in the saddle' or 'on the back of the saddle' and completes the temporal/location phrase.
segments.s069-g212: Grammatically this reads as a copular clause: "(پشت زین) مرا تختگاه است" — 'the saddle's back is my bed/seat.' "مرا" functions as 'for me' (possessive/ethical dative), "تختگاه" is 'seat/bed,' and "است" is the copula. The conjunction "و" links the second verbless (stative) clause "اسپم زمین" where "اسپم" = 'my steed' and "زمین" = 'the ground' — i.e. 'my horse lies on the ground' (understood statively, implying it is down/dead or on the earth).
segments.s069-g213: "همه" is the quantifier 'all' modifying the coordinated noun phrase. "گرگساران" is a collective/pejorative term ('wolf-people' / brigands) and "مازنداران" refers to people of Mazandaran (here plural). The phrase lists the groups the speaker set in motion.
segments.s069-g214: "به تو" marks direction/target 'toward you' (i.e. against you). "راست کردم" literally 'I made/turned (them) straight' — idiomatically 'I sent/aimed them directly' (past tense, first person). The final prepositional phrase "به گرز گران" expresses instrument/means ('with a heavy mace' or 'bearing heavy maces'), where "گرز" is 'mace' and "گران" 'heavy/weighty.'
segments.s069-g215: "نکردم" is the negative past-tense first-person verb 'I did not (do).' "زمانی" here functions adverbially as 'ever / at any time' (used with the negation to mean 'never'). "برو بوم" is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'in my homeland / in the native land.' "یاد" is the noun 'remembrance' — together: 'I did not at any time (in my land) lose the memory (of you).' This clause supplies the object that continues into the next segment.
segments.s069-g216: "ترا" (here shown split as "ت" + "را") is the accusative 'you.' "خواستم" is past 'I wished / I desired' and takes a predicative complement; the three adjectives "راد و پیروز و شاد" ('generous / noble', 'victorious', 'happy') state what the speaker wished for the addressee.
