Word order places the object before the verb, a common classical Persian pattern.
segments.s069-g169: Literal order: subject (جهان) + locative adjunct (پیش چشمم) + simile (چو دریا) + verb (نمود). پیش چشمم is a prepositional/locative phrase 'before my eye(s)' where م is the 1st-person possessive clitic attached to چشم; چو introduces a simile 'like/as'. نمود is a past tense verb 'appeared'.
segments.s069-g170: This phrase describes the smoke (دود) and uses a small prepositional/transformative cluster: به + ابر سیه + بر شده + تیره دود. بر شده is a compound meaning 'having risen / become upon', so together the phrase modifies دود: 'dark smoke that had become (or hung) as a black cloud.' تیره modifies دود ('dark/black smoke').
segments.s069-g171: ز here means 'from/because of' (causal). بانگش (بانگ + -ش) is 'his cry/voice' (possessive clitic). بلرزید is past tense 'trembled/was shaken'. روی زمین is a locative expression 'on the surface of the earth' placed after the verb for emphasis: 'the surface of the earth trembled.'
segments.s069-g172: Again ز introduces cause 'because of / from'. زهرش (زهر + -ش) 'his poison/venom' is figurative here (the deadly effect of his presence/voice). زمین شد is a simple past passive/mediopassive 'the earth became' or 'the ground was made', and چو introduces a simile: 'became like a sea (دریا) of چین (wrinkles/folds)'.
segments.s069-g173: This clause contains imperatives and a light-verb construction. برو is an imperative 'go' (addressing someone). بر زدم بانگ is a verbal phrase in which بر + زدن with بانگ as object means 'to raise/utter a cry' (زدم is past tense 'I struck/uttered' or used idiomatically 'I raised'). برسان is an imperative 'bring/deliver', and شیر is vocative 'O lion'. Word order is terse/poetic; imperative verbs frame the action.
segments.s069-g174: چنان چون is an emphatic pair meaning 'such/so' (چنان) + 'as' (چون); بود is the past form 'was'. کار مرد دلیر is the noun phrase 'the deed/work of the brave man' (کار = deed; مرد دلیر = brave man/hero). The predicate (چنان چون بود) follows the noun phrase for rhetorical effect: 'such was the deed of the brave man.'
segments.s069-g175: This is a compact noun phrase enumerating a single projectile with emphatic epithets: یکی 'a certain/one', تیر and پیکان both 'arrow/shaft' (two synonyms), الماس qualifies the arrow as 'diamond' (precious/hard), and خدنگ is an epithet meaning a dagger-like or pointed weapon. The sequence lists the object and its attributes in apposition.
segments.s069-g176: به + چرخ اندرون is a directional phrase 'into/toward the sky' (چرخ = sky/heaven, اندرون = within). راندم is past tense 'I drove/hurled/launched' (from راندن), and بی‌درنگ is an adverb 'without delay / immediately.' The verb follows the directional phrase, yielding 'I launched into the sky immediately.'
segments.s069-g185: به نیروی introduces an instrumental phrase 'by/with the power (نیروی) of ...'. Here نیروی takes the possessor یزدان ('of God'), and گیهان خدای is an epithet 'lord/master of the world' following the name—an archaic/poetic appositive ordering. The preposition به is functioning as 'by' (instrumental).
segments.s069-g186: برانگیختم is a past, first-person, transitive verb 'I raised/roused'. پیلتن را is the definite direct object marked by را. ز جای is a prepositional phrase 'from (his) place' with ز = 'from' and جای = 'place'. Word order here places the verb before the object and the source phrase.
segments.s069-g187: زدم is the past first-person 'I struck'. بر سرش is a prepositional phrase 'upon his head', with the enclitic ش marking 'his'. گرزه گاو چهر is a compound noun phrase 'mace (گرزه) of/carrying a cow-face' where the two nouns act as a descriptive phrase following the head in poetic word order.
segments.s069-g188: برو here is an archaic/poetic contraction of بر او ('upon him') rather than the imperative 'go'; it governs کوه بارید 'mountains fell'. بارید is a past tense used figuratively 'fell/downpoured'. گفتی سپهر is a brief clause meaning 'the sky seemed to say / the heavens seemed to pour' — گفتی is used idiomatically/poetically and does not match the simple modern senses given, so its exact grammatical shade is idiomatic.
segments.s069-g189: شکستم is a past transitive 'I broke/smashed'. سرش is 'his head' (سر + enclitic ش). چون introduces a simile 'like', followed by تن ژنده پیل 'the body of a ragged elephant' (ژنده qualifying تن). The clause compares the smashed head to that gross image.
segments.s069-g190: فرو ریخت is a compound verbal idea 'poured down/flowed downward' (فرو = down(ward) + ریخت = poured). ز (here 'from') marks source (here 'from him'), and زهر is literal 'poison' used figuratively (blood/filth) followed by a simile introduced by چون رود نیل 'like the Nile river' (رود نیل = the Nile).
segments.s069-g191: به زخمی چنان شد is 'he became so (چنان شد) with a wound'—به here introduces the condition ('with/by a wound'). که دیگر نخاست is a result clause 'that he would no longer rise'; نخاست is past negative 'did not rise' (i.e. could not/get up no more).
segments.s069-g192: ز مغزش 'from his brain' (ز = from + مغزش = his brain). زمین گشت 'the earth became' (گشت = became/turned), and باکوه راست is a compact phrase meaning 'even/level with the mountain' (باکوه = with/against the mountain; راست = straight/level). The clause describes the aftermath — the ground and the mountain being made even by what poured from his head.
segments.s069-g193: کشف رود is the noun phrase serving as the subject; in poetic usage کشف (here literal 'uncovering/bed' or exposed part) with رود ('river') yields 'the river' or 'the river's exposed bed'. پر + noun (خون و زرداب) is a predicative expression 'full of ...', followed by the past copula شد 'became'. Word order is subject — predicate — copula (poetic), so the whole means 'the river became full of blood and mire.'
segments.s069-g194: زمین is the subject 'the earth/ground'; جای is a predicate noun 'a place' and آرامش و خواب are coordinated complements 'tranquility and sleep' that follow جای. The copula شد at the end gives the past-state meaning 'became a place of rest and sleep.' The phrase is a standard nominal predicate with the copula postponed for poetic rhythm.
segments.s069-g195: همه کوهساران is a plural subject 'all the mountains / all the mountain-dwellers'; پر از is the adjectival construction 'full of' and takes the following noun phrase مرد و زن as its complement. The preposition/particle از pairs with پر to mean 'full of'.
segments.s069-g196: همی is an iterative/adverbial particle meaning 'continually' or 'repeatedly'; آفرین here is the interjection/acclamation 'bravo' (not the verb 'to create'); خواندندی is an archaic/poetic past plural verb form equivalent to خواندند 'they uttered/cried' (the -ی ending is an older inflectional form). بمن is the prepositional phrase 'to me'. Altogether: 'they repeatedly cried “bravo” to me.'
segments.s069-g197: جهانی here denotes 'the whole world' or 'everyone'; بران is a poetic locative/relational particle meaning 'upon/at that' and links to جنگ 'that battle'. نظاره is a noun 'watching/observation' and بود is the past copula 'was' — together: 'the whole world was watching that battle.' The order is emphatic/poetic (subject — locative phrase — object — verbal copula).
segments.s069-g198: که here functions as a conjunction meaning 'for/because' introducing an explanatory clause. آن اژدها is 'that dragon'; زشت is the adjective 'ugly' and پتیاره is an archaic descriptor (rare/old-fashioned adjective modifying the dragon). بود is the past copula 'was'. The clause explains why the world watched: 'for that dragon was ugly/pitiable.'
segments.s069-g199: مرا (here written as م + را in the word list) is the direct object 'me' (object marker را attached); سام is the predicate name — together 'they called me Sam' — خواندند is the past plural verb 'they called/named'. The phrase یک زخم ازان is an inserted phrase 'one wound from that' (ازان = 'from that') giving a reason or circumscribing detail: 'they called me Sam on account of that one wound.' Word order places the object and name before the verb, as is common in epic/persuasive diction.
segments.s069-g200: جهان is the subject 'the world' (understood collectively as people), زر و گوهر are the direct objects 'gold and jewels', and برافشاندند is the past plural verb 'they scattered/ showered'. The verb at the end gives the sense 'the world showered gold and gems.' This ordering (subject — objects — verb) is normal in poetry and emphasizes the scattered treasure.
segments.s069-g57: مگر at the start introduces an exception (‘except’). من is the subject; که is a relative particle meaning ‘who/that’ introducing the relative clause. از داد بی‌بهره‌ام literally orders as preposition + noun + adjectival predicate: از + داد = ‘of/from justice’ and بی‌بهره‌ام is an adjective with the first‑person suffix -ام (‘I am [deprived]’), so the clause reads ‘who am deprived of justice.’
segments.s069-g58: و گرچه is a pair of conjunctions (‘and, although’), signaling a concessive clause. به پیوند تو is a prepositional phrase (‘by/through your tie/connection’) placed before the predicate; شهره‌ام is formed from شهره (fame/famous) plus the first‑person suffix -ام and functions as a predicate ‘I am famed.’ The construction means ‘and although I am famed by my connection to you.’
segments.s069-g59: یکی here is indefinite ‘a certain/one’; مرغ پرورده‌ام is a noun phrase where پرورده (a participle/adjectival past form of ‘to rear’) carries the possessive suffix -ام, i.e. ‘a bird of mine that I reared’ or ‘one bird I had reared.’ خاک خورد is an idiomatic verb phrase (object before verb in classical order): خاک ‘dust/earth’ + خورد ‘ate’ = ‘ate dust’ (i.e. fell/was humbled/was defeated).
segments.s069-g60: به گیتی is a locative prepositional phrase (‘in/throughout the world’). The sequence مرا نیست is an older/expressional word order meaning roughly ‘for me there is not’ (مرا here marks the person concerned), followed by با کس نبرد (‘to fight with anyone’). The whole clause means ‘there is no one in the world who fights with me’ — i.e. ‘no one can stand against me.’
segments.s069-g61: ندانم here functions as ‘I do not deem/consider’ (an idiomatic use of the verb ‘to know’ = ‘to consider’). همی is a classical emphatic/continuative particle. خویشتن را places ‘myself’ as the marked object/topic and گناه is the nominal predicate ‘guilt/blame’: together ‘I do not consider myself guilty.’
segments.s069-g62: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause (‘for/because’). بر من = ‘upon/against me’ (shows who is affected). کسی را (marked by را) = ‘someone’ as the patient/agent in the subordinate clause. بران is an archaic/formal particle/word (here functioning adverbially/relationally) and هست is the copula ‘is/exist’; راه = ‘path/way.’ Together the clause gives the reason: ‘for someone has (put me) upon that way,’ i.e. ‘someone set me on that path’ or ‘there is someone who bears responsibility,’ which explains why he does not blame himself.
segments.s069-g63: مگر آنکه is a stock classical phrase meaning ‘except that.’ سام is the proper name; یلستم is an epithet attached to Sam (a poetic/archaic epithet meaning something like ‘mighty among heroes’ here) and پدر is the noun ‘father’ in apposition — the copula is omitted in classical style. The phrase reads ‘except that Sam Yelestam is (my) father.’
segments.s069-g64: و‌گر هست = ‘and if there is’ (وگر = ‘if/but if’, هست = ‘there is’). با این نژادم is a prepositional phrase ‘with/in this lineage’ (نژادم = ‘my lineage’ with the possessive suffix -م). هنر is the predicate meaning ‘virtue/excellence’ (here heroic prowess). Together: ‘and if there is any excellence in this lineage.’
segments.s069-g233: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'since'). از رای او is a prepositional phrase 'from his counsel/plan' (word order: از + noun + pronoun). سر نپیچم is a first-person negative present form built on پیچیدن with the negative prefix ن- (نپیچم = 'I do not/ will not turn'), where سر (literally 'head') is the direct object/part of the idiom meaning 'turn away' or 'deviate'; به هیچ ('for nothing/for no reason') modifies نپیچم and intensifies the negation.
segments.s069-g234: درین (در + این) = 'in these' (در = in, ین = the demonstrative 'this' written archaically). روزها is plural 'days' used temporally ('these days'). کرد is the simple past 'he/she did/made' (3rd sg) and links back to the previously mentioned agent; زی is an archaic preposition (from/of/onto) here marking the relation to من (me) and بسیچ is a quantifier 'many'/'much' describing what was done to me.
segments.s069-g235: به پیش من آمد = 'came before me / came into my presence' (به + پیش + object + آمد). پر از خون is a postnominal adjectival phrase 'full of blood' (پر = 'full (of)' + از خون = 'of blood'). رخان is a poetic adjective modifying the blood phrase (here used metaphorically to suggest loose/flowing blood).
segments.s069-g236: همی is an adverb of continuity ('kept / continually'); the reduplicated چاک چاک is an onomatopoetic intensifier meaning 'torn / gaping, again and again'. آمدش is the past form آمد with the enclitic -ش ('to/for him' or a clitic referring to the person) used idiomatically here with an intransitive sense 'came' or 'issued'; ز استخوان 'from the bones' is a source phrase showing where the gashes/blood came from.
segments.s069-g237: مرا گفت = 'he said to me' (مرا as the object before the verb). بردار is an imperative 'take' (here giving an instruction) and آمل کنی combines the place name آمل with a subjunctive/optative کنی ('that you do/take to Amol'), i.e. an indirect speech construction where the speaker reports the instruction to 'take (to) Amol.'
segments.s069-g238: سزاتر is a comparative/adverbial form meaning 'more fitting / more proper' (from سزا in the sense of 'desert/appropriateness'). که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. آهنگ کابل کنی literally 'you make an intention/tendency toward Kabul' (آهنگ = intention/aim; کابل = Kabul), with کنی the present-subjunctive/optative 'you should/might do', giving advice or recommendation.
segments.s069-g239: چو = 'as/like' (introducing a simile). پرورده مرغ = 'a reared / tamed bird' (adjective + noun). باشد is the present/subjunctive third-person form 'is / may be' used here to state the likeness; به کوه = 'on the mountain' (the locative phrase follows the predicate).
segments.s069-g240: نشانی شده is a perfect/passive participial phrase 'having been made/placed as a sign' (نشانی = sign/mark; شده = become/made). در میان گروه = 'in the middle of / among the company' (در میان = among; گروه = group/troop), completing the descriptive simile.
segments.s069-g217: کنون is an adverbial fronting 'now/presently'. این + برافراخته + یال من = 'this raised/bristling mane of mine': برافراخته is an adjective (here placed before the noun for poetic emphasis), یال is the noun 'mane', and من follows the noun as the possessive 'of me/my'. Word order is topicalized for emphasis: time → demonstrative + adjective + noun + possessor.
segments.s069-g218: همان is a demonstrative/emphatic 'that very' pointing back; زخم is the head noun 'wound'. کوبنده is an active/adjectival participle 'striking/battering' modifying زخم (post‑ or pre‑nominal order can vary in poetry). کوپال من groups as the possessor/agent phrase 'my Koopal' (Koopal being a named weapon/person) so the whole noun phrase reads 'that very striking/battering wound of my Koopal.'
segments.s069-g219: بدان here functions as an imperative/pointing 'know/consider that' (ب as a prefix + دان 'know'); هم is emphatic 'also/even'; که introduces the relative clause 'that which/which you'; بودی is the past form 'you were'; نماند is past 'did not remain/was not left'. همی is an emphatic/continuative particle reinforcing the statement. The clause overall: 'Know also that what you were did not remain.'
segments.s069-g220: بر is a locative/prepositional particle here ('on/over' or 'as for'); و simply 'and' joining elements; گردگاهم is 'my neck' (گردگاه + possessive -am); خماند is a past/intransitive verb 'remained/was left' (used here of the body part) and همی is again an emphatic/continuous particle. Read together as a comment on the state of the speaker's neck (e.g. 'and my neck remained thus').
segments.s069-g221: کمندی (a snare/lasso) is the direct object; بینداخت is the simple past 'threw/cast'. از دست is a prepositional phrase 'from/by the hand' (از + دست). شست here is a poetic/elliptical element attached to دست in the manuscript division (literally 'thumb'), used in formulaic/poetic phrasing to indicate the agency of the hand; together the phrase conveys 'with/by the hand (of fate) a snare was cast.'
segments.s069-g222: زمانه is the subject 'time/fortune'; مرا is split in the edition into م + را (m + object marker) but functions together as 'me' (accusative). باژگونه is an adverb/adjective 'upside‑down/inverted'; ببست is past tense 'bound/tied' (3rd person). The clause: 'Time bound me upside‑down.'
segments.s069-g223: سپردیم is 1st‑person plural past 'we entrusted/handed over'; نوبت 'turn/occasion' is the thing handed; کنون 'now' is a temporal adverb; زال را is the object phrase 'Zal' marked with را (object/definite marker) — literally 'we handed over the turn now to Zal.' Word order places verb first, then the noun phrases for emphasis.
segments.s069-g224: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/so that'); شاید is a modal adverb 'perhaps/it may be'; کمربند و کوپال را lists two definite objects 'the belt and Koopal' followed by را preparing them as objects of an implicit or upcoming verb. The clause is anticipatory/optative: 'that perhaps the belt and Koopal ...' (the sense continues in the next lines).
